title: Future Census Independent Evaluation Panel | Stats NZ
content:
## Future Census Independent Evaluation Panel
19 September 2024, 2:30pm
The Future Census Independent Evaluation Panel was convened by the Government Statistician in July 2024 to provide an independent review and advice on the approach to the next census in Aotearoa New Zealand.
Stats NZ is exploring options for increasing the use of administrative data, supplemented by surveying and tailored data collection approaches, to deliver the census from 2028 and beyond.
The panel was convened to enable a robust, defendable, and fulsome review, evaluation, and recommendation process.
Future Census Independent External Review Panel terms of reference has more information about the panel’s role, responsibilities and scope.
The Independent Evaluation Panel members represent a diverse range of data user perspectives, including expertise from academia, commerce and technology, central government, disability, international perspectives, Rainbow, Pacific, and iwi Māori.

### Dr. Jonathan Godfrey ONZM (Panel Chair)
Role: National President – Blind Citizens NZ, Senior Lecturer in Statistics – Massey University
Dr. Jonathan Godfrey is a leading advocate for disability rights, specialising in disability data and upholding the rights of disabled individuals. He led the Independent Monitoring Mechanism Delegation to Geneva in 2022 for New Zealand’s review by the UN Committee on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities. Jonathan’s expertise means he has worked with various government agencies, including Stats NZ’s Data Ethics Advisory Group.
As the first totally blind person to become a lecturer in statistics globally, Jonathan teaches undergraduate courses and supervises graduate students at Massey University. His research focuses on developing tools to help blind individuals navigate visual elements of statistics and offers statistical advice to staff and students.
Role: Assistant Chief Statistician – Statistics Canada
Geoff Bowlby has been an executive with Statistics Canada for 17 years, currently serving as the Assistant Chief Statistician for Census, Regional Services, and Operations Field. Previously, he managed the pandemic-impacted 2021 Census, and oversaw data collection operations and regional services. Geoff also directed the Labour Force Survey and managed various special surveys.
Geoff has testified before parliamentary committees and chaired UN task forces. He is currently leading two UN task forces on updating international census manuals. Geoff holds a BA and MA in Economics and completed a Certificate in Public Sector Leadership from the University of Ottawa in 2020.

### Grace Campbell-Macdonald
Role: Director Regulatory and Advisory – National Cyber Security Centre
Grace Campbell-Macdonald is a public servant with 20-years’ experience in both policy and operational roles across a range of government agencies. Grace has an interest in the timeliness and utility of the data of Future Census to inform analysis, advice, and service design. She also has an interest in how Census data is secured so that trust and confidence in government systems is not undermined.
Role: Senior Research Fellow – Te Ngira Institute for Population Research, University of Waikato
Dr. Jesse Whitehead has a primary focus on health equity and population health in his work. Jesse’s interdisciplinary research integrates insights from fields such as populations studies, demography, rural health, and Geographic Information Systems. He co-chairs the Hauora Taiwhenau Rural Health Network Rural Health Research and Education Chapter and contributed to the development of the Geographic Classification for Health.
Jesse’s current research interests include working to support the development of resilient and equitable health systems through refining measures of spatial equity and access to health and social services and understanding demographic change in a changing environment.

### Malaetogia Jacinta Fa’alili-Fidow
Role: Director Strategy – Moana Connect
Maletogia Jacinta Fa’alili-Fidow has a background in public health. She is the Director, Strategy of Moana Connect, working with an incredible team undertaking various projects in the health and social sectors. Jacinta also recently completed a PhD on Pacific child resilience pathways titled ‘Vā o Tamaiti’.

### Moira Clunie MNZM (Te Rarawa)
Role: Project Lead – Te Ngākau Kahukura
Moira Clunie is dedicated to creating safer and more inclusive environments for rainbow/LGBTI+ people in Aotearoa. As Project Lead for Te Ngākau Kahukura, they work on improving systems so rainbow individuals feel safe, valued, and included in their communities, education, and healthcare.
Moira has contributed to Stats NZ’s gender and sex data standards and collaborates with various agencies and researchers to enhance rainbow data. They are an Associate Investigator for the Counting Ourselves survey, and co-chair the Gender Affirming Primary Care Advisory Group at Te Whatu Ora Health New Zealand. They also volunteer as co-chair of the PATHA Policy and Advocacy Committee. Moira holds a Master of Social and Community Leadership, focusing on rainbow community leadership in suicide prevention.
Role: Chief Executive Officer – Figure.NZ
Ngapera Riley is the leader of Figure.NZ, a charity that builds scalable technology to help democratise New Zealand’s public data and develops data literacy programs for charities and NGOs. She works closely with Māori business and education efforts across Aotearoa New Zealand. Ngapera currently holds board positions with the Simplicity Trust, Inzone Education Foundation, and Te Ohu Whai Ao Trust, and Te Kaenga Hou Trust.
Ngapera is also part of the working group Te Pokapu for the Māori Data Sovereignty Network. She has worked in an advisory capacity for Stats NZ on several projects including Māori business definitions and Tatauranga Umanga Māori, and Aotearoa Data Explorer.

### Philippa Howden-Chapman CNZM QSO
Role: Professor of Public Health – University of Otago
Philippa Howden-Chapman is a Distinguished Professor of Public Health at the University of Otago, Wellington. She is Co-Director of He Kāinga Oranga/ Housing and Health Research Programme and Director of the NZ Centre for Sustainable Cities. She leads research on housing and health. Her community housing trials have greatly impacted housing, health, and energy policies. Philippa focuses on reducing health inequalities and has received several awards for her work.
In 2014, Philippa won the Prime Minister’s Science Prize, becoming the first woman and social scientist to do so. In 2021, she received the Royal Society of NZ Rutherford Medal. She has chaired and participated in various high-profile health and housing committees and is a Fellow of the Royal Society of New Zealand.

### Russell Craig, MInstD
Role: Independent Data and Digital Technology Expert Advisor
Russell Craig is a specialist in cloud computing, AI, data governance, digital identity, and the intersection of technology and public policy. He has had a global career advising governments, businesses, and non-profits on maximizing their digital technology investments.
From 2014 to 2023, Russell served as Microsoft New Zealand’s National Technology and Security Officer, guiding public and private sectors on digital strategy and risk management. He now focuses on the ethical use of data and technology, helping New Zealand organizations enhance their data governance and value. He also serves on Stats NZ’s Data Ethics Advisory Group.
Role: Data Analyst – Ngāti Rangi
Tanya Albert has been the lead data analyst at Ngāti Rangi for six years, specialising in data collection, cleaning, and analysis. She aims to use data-driven insights to positively impact whānau wellbeing and support iwi aspirations through informed decision-making. Tanya is skilled in troubleshooting database issues and improving data systems.
She is involved in He Aho Kura Whakina, an IDI research program with Te Kāhui Raraunga, and is pursuing a micro-credential in digital health through Health Informatics NZ. Tanya has collaborated with Stats NZ and iwi data analysts to promote data-driven decision-making and is an expert in the Whānau Tahi database. Tanya’s work is driven by a commitment to using data for the benefit of iwi, hapori, and whānau.
- Independent panel recommendations 19 September 2024 Image: Future Census Independent Evaluation Panel
